stories.headline: NASA's Curiosity Rover Finds 'Spiderweb' Rocks That Rewrote Mars' Water History
stories.hook: Bizarre boxwork formations on the Red Planet suggest groundwater lingered far longer than anyone expected.
stories.body:
For the past six months, NASA's Curiosity rover has been crawling through one of the most visually striking landscapes it has encountered in over a decade on Mars: a field of low, interlocking ridges that look unmistakably like a giant's spiderweb. Standing roughly 3 to 6 feet tall, these 'boxwork' formations appear to have formed when mineral-rich groundwater percolated through cracks in the rock, hardening into ridges while the surrounding softer material eroded away over billions of years.

Up close, the rover's instruments revealed something even more surprising. The formations contain clay minerals and carbonate compounds — strong chemical signatures of prolonged liquid water interaction. Scientists were especially struck by the fact that these structures appear so high up the flanks of Mount Sharp, a layered mountain inside Gale Crater. 'Seeing boxwork this far up the mountain suggests the groundwater table had to be pretty high,' said one researcher on the Curiosity team, implying that habitable conditions may have persisted on Mars for far longer than orbital surveys had suggested.

The discovery is reshaping ideas about when and how Mars transitioned from a potentially habitable world to the frozen desert it is today. Curiosity is expected to move on from the boxwork region in mid-March 2026, but the data it collected will keep researchers busy for years. The findings add to a growing body of evidence — including ancient sand dunes in Gale Crater that appear to have been soaked by underground water — that Mars' wet chapter may not have ended as abruptly as once believed.

image.alt: Surreal desert sand dune landscape at sunset with red-orange tones
image.credit: Photo by and machines on Unsplash

links.title: NASA JPL: Curiosity Rover Sees Martian 'Spiderwebs' Up Close
Live Science: Giant 'spiderwebs' on Mars contain egg-like structures
EarthSky: Giant 'spiderwebs' on Mars reveal surprising water clues

stories.headline: Artemis II Finally Has a Launch Date: April 1 Will Send Humans Around the Moon
stories.hook: For the first time in 54 years, human beings will leave Earth orbit — if everything holds together.
stories.body:
NASA confirmed this week that Artemis II, the first crewed mission of the Artemis program, will launch no earlier than April 1, 2026, from Kennedy Space Center in Florida. The 10-day mission will carry four astronauts — Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen — on a free-return trajectory around the Moon before splashing down in the Pacific Ocean. It will be the first time humans have ventured beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in December 1972.

The path to April 1 hasn't been smooth. NASA had originally hoped to launch earlier in the year, but engineers discovered an issue with the rocket's helium pressurization system during a fueling test in February, forcing the Space Launch System (SLS) rocket to be rolled back into the Vehicle Assembly Building for repairs. After completing a flight readiness review, officials announced the rocket will roll back to the launch pad around March 19 for final countdown preparations.

The mission is technically a lunar flyby rather than a landing — Artemis III is now scheduled to be the first crewed lunar landing, targeting 2027 or 2028. Still, the significance of Artemis II is hard to overstate. It will test the Orion crew capsule's life support, navigation, and re-entry systems with people actually on board, paving the way for NASA's long-term goal of a sustainable human presence at the Moon's south pole.

image.alt: Rocket trail blazing across the night sky above city buildings
image.credit: Photo by Johnathan Kaufman on Unsplash

links.title: NPR: NASA sets Artemis II launch for April 1
NASA: Artemis II Mission Overview
Space.com: Top spaceflight moments to look forward to in 2026

stories.headline: GPT-5.4 Just Became the First AI to Outperform Humans at Using a Computer
stories.hook: OpenAI's new model hit 75% on a benchmark where the average human scores 72.4% — and the gap is only going to grow.
stories.body:
On March 5, 2026, OpenAI released GPT-5.4, and buried in the launch announcement was a milestone that had been years in the making: an AI model had, for the first time, demonstrably surpassed the average human on a standardized test of desktop computer use. On OSWorld-Verified — a benchmark that measures how well a model can navigate a real operating system through screenshots, mouse clicks, and keyboard actions — GPT-5.4 scored 75.0%, clearing the human baseline of 72.4% and leaving its predecessor, GPT-5.2, far behind at 47.3%.

The model brings together several capabilities that were previously separate: long-horizon planning, deep web research, code execution, and now native computer use, all in one system. It supports a context window of up to one million tokens and introduces a tool-search mechanism that cuts token costs in complex workflows by 47% without any accuracy penalty. Professional benchmark scores sit at 83%, making it the most capable model OpenAI has shipped for workplace tasks.

The timing is noteworthy. Morgan Stanley published analysis this week warning that a 'transformative leap' in AI is now imminent, driven by 10x increases in training compute at the leading labs. Competitors aren't standing still either — Google's Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite launched the same week targeting high-volume developer use cases. But GPT-5.4's crossing of the human threshold on computer use is the kind of concrete, measurable benchmark that tends to shift how both researchers and the general public think about what AI systems can actually do.

image.alt: Futuristic AI microchip resting on a glossy surface with colorful reflections
image.credit: Photo by Omar:. Lopez-Rincon on Unsplash

links.title: OpenAI: Introducing GPT-5.4
TechCrunch: OpenAI launches GPT-5.4 with Pro and Thinking versions
Fortune: Morgan Stanley warns an AI breakthrough is coming in 2026

stories.headline: Astronomers Pinpointed the Brightest Fast Radio Burst Ever Detected to a Single Spiral Arm
stories.hook: A flash that lasted a fraction of a second briefly outshone every radio source in its galaxy — and scientists now know exactly where it came from.
stories.body:
Fast radio bursts are among the most energetic and mysterious phenomena in the known universe: millisecond flashes of radio waves whose origins have puzzled astronomers since they were first detected in 2007. A new burst, catalogued FRB 20250316A and nicknamed RBFLOAT ('Radio Brightest Flash Of All Time'), has just set a new record — and for the first time, researchers have pinpointed one of these events with enough precision to trace it to a specific structure within its host galaxy.

Using the completed network of CHIME/FRB Outrigger telescopes — smaller versions of the main CHIME instrument installed at sites in British Columbia, West Virginia, and California — the team localized RBFLOAT to a single spiral arm of NGC 4141, a galaxy about 130 million light-years from Earth. The localization was accurate to within just 42 light-years, an extraordinary feat for an event in a galaxy that distant. The burst was observed on March 16 and its brightness was so extreme that it briefly outshone every other radio source in NGC 4141.

Perhaps most intriguingly, the precise location allowed follow-up observations with the James Webb Space Telescope, which detected a faint infrared signal at the exact spot. Whether that signal represents the burst's aftermath, a persistent counterpart, or an unrelated source is now a major focus of investigation. Fast radio bursts are thought to originate from magnetars — the incredibly dense remnants of massive stars with ferociously strong magnetic fields — but the diversity of environments in which they occur continues to challenge simple models.

image.alt: Large satellite dish pointing skyward on a metal structure
image.credit: Photo by Ambitious Studio* | Rick Barrett on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Astronomers just found the source of the brightest fast radio burst ever
MIT News: Astronomers detect the brightest fast radio burst of all time

stories.headline: Hundreds of Energy Enzymes Are Living Inside Your Cell Nuclei — and Nobody Knew
stories.body:
Biology textbooks have long described a clean division of labor inside cells: mitochondria handle energy production, and the nucleus handles DNA. A new study published in Nature Communications just threw a wrench into that picture, revealing that more than 200 metabolic enzymes — the molecular machines typically associated with making energy in mitochondria — are also found directly on chromatin, the DNA-protein complex inside the cell nucleus.

About 7% of all proteins attached to chromatin turned out to be metabolic enzymes, forming what researchers are calling a 'mini metabolism' inside the nucleus. Even stranger: many of these enzymes are physically larger than the pores through which they would need to pass to enter the nucleus, suggesting cells have an as-yet-unknown mechanism for getting them inside. When DNA damage occurs, some of these enzymes appear to migrate specifically toward the damaged regions, apparently assisting in repair.

The implications for cancer research are particularly striking. Different tissues and cancers show distinct patterns of which enzymes are present on their DNA, creating what the team calls a 'nuclear metabolic fingerprint.' This fingerprint could eventually be used to classify cancer types or predict how tumors will respond to treatment. It also raises a deeper question about whether metabolism and gene regulation are far more intertwined than scientists have appreciated — a connection that could reshape how we think about everything from aging to the development of new drugs.
stories.hook: More than 200 energy-producing enzymes have been found sitting directly on human DNA — a discovery that rewrites a basic principle of cell biology.

image.alt: Colorful microscopic cross-section of plant cells showing cellular structure
image.credit: Photo by Bioscience Image Library by Fayette Reynolds on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Hidden metabolism found operating inside the cell nucleus
SciTechDaily: Scientists Discover Hundreds of Energy Enzymes Sitting Directly on Human DNA

stories.headline: Textbooks Were Wrong: Hair Doesn't Get Pushed Out — It Gets Pulled
stories.hook: A spiral dance of cells inside your hair follicle is the real motor of hair growth, and the discovery could transform how we treat hair loss.
stories.body:
The biology of hair growth has been taught the same way for decades: cells divide at the base of the follicle and push the hair shaft upward, like toothpaste squeezed from a tube. New research from L'Oréal Research & Innovation and Queen Mary University of London, published in Nature Communications, shows that picture is almost entirely backward. Hair isn't pushed — it's pulled, by a spiral movement of cells in the outer root sheath that acts like a microscopic motor.

Using advanced 3D live imaging techniques, the team observed individual cells moving in a helical pattern downward within the same region where the upward pulling force is generated. When researchers disrupted actin — a protein responsible for cellular movement — hair growth slowed by more than 80%, confirming that this cell migration is the primary driver of growth rather than a secondary effect. The outer root sheath, long considered a structural scaffold for the follicle, turns out to be an active, mechanically essential component.

A separate but related discovery, published around the same time, identified a previously unknown 'third cell type' — accessory mesenchymal cells — that appears to be essential for regrowing fully functional hair follicles in the lab. Scientists had previously been unable to fully recreate the hair growth cycle in culture; adding this third cell type allowed follicles to complete the entire cycle and produce a visible hair shaft within two weeks. Together, the two discoveries suggest that hair loss treatments based on stem cells or follicle regeneration may have been missing a crucial piece of the puzzle.

image.alt: Black and white scanning electron microscope image of microscopic biological structures
image.credit: Photo by Mathew Schwartz on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Textbooks were wrong — scientists reveal how human hair really grows
Phys.org: Newfound third cell type enables fully functional hair follicles in the lab

stories.headline: Your Salad Might Contain Antidepressants. Here's Why That's More Complicated Than It Sounds
stories.hook: New research confirms that crops irrigated with treated wastewater absorb pharmaceuticals into their leaves — but the edible parts may be safer than you think.
stories.body:
As freshwater becomes scarcer in many parts of the world, treated municipal wastewater is increasingly being used to irrigate crops. Regulators and consumers have long worried about one uncomfortable byproduct of that practice: trace amounts of pharmaceuticals — flushed or excreted by patients and only partially removed by treatment plants — winding up in food. A new study from Johns Hopkins University, published in Environmental Science and Technology, has now tracked exactly where those drugs end up in common vegetables, and the results are more nuanced than alarm might suggest.

Researchers studied four psychoactive medications frequently found in treated wastewater — carbamazepine (epilepsy and bipolar disorder), lamotrigine (epilepsy), amitriptyline (depression), and fluoxetine (depression). In tomatoes, carrots, and lettuce, the compounds tended to accumulate primarily in the leaves rather than in the parts humans eat. For tomatoes and carrots, this is good news: you eat the fruit and the root, not the leaves. Lettuce is more complicated, since the leaves are the point — though concentration levels were still very low.

One compound stood out: carbamazepine accumulated more broadly across plant tissues, including in carrot roots and tomato fruit, at higher concentrations than the others. The researchers note that the absolute amounts detected are still far below therapeutic doses, but they stress that the long-term effects of chronic low-level exposure remain understudied. The findings underscore both the resilience of our food system and the genuine need for more sophisticated wastewater treatment technologies as recycled water use expands globally.

image.alt: Green pumpkin plants growing in rich dark soil
image.credit: Photo by Jason Mitrione on Unsplash

links.title: Johns Hopkins Hub: Crops store pharmaceutical byproducts in their leaves
Phys.org: Good news for wastewater irrigation — three crops store pharmaceutical byproducts in leaves

stories.headline: Could Solar Flares Actually Trigger Earthquakes? A Kyoto Team Built a Model That Says Maybe
stories.hook: A controversial new study proposes that solar activity can charge up Earth's fault zones like a capacitor — and occasionally push them over the edge.
stories.body:
It sounds like the premise of a disaster movie: a solar flare erupts, disturbs the ionosphere 60 miles above Earth, and somewhere far below, a critically stressed fault slips. Yet a theoretical model published in February 2026 by researchers at Kyoto University argues this chain of events is physically plausible — and possibly even measurable. The paper, published in the International Journal of Plasma Environmental Science and Technology, describes how electric fields generated by ionospheric disturbances could penetrate deep into the crust and apply pressure to fracture zones already near their breaking point.

The proposed mechanism relies on a specific picture of cracked rock: fracture zones filled with superheated, supercritical water under enormous pressure, which may behave electrically like capacitors. In this model, the ionosphere and the crustal voids form a coupled electrostatic system, and when a solar flare kicks the ionosphere hard enough, it adds a few extra megapascals of pressure — potentially the straw that breaks the camel's back for a fault that was already critically loaded. The team points to the 2024 Noto Peninsula earthquake in Japan, which occurred shortly after a period of intense solar activity, as a circumstantially suggestive case.

Other researchers are skeptical. The model is explicitly theoretical, relies on several simplifying assumptions, and real-world geology may dampen the effects to insignificance before they reach fault zones. The paper's authors themselves acknowledge they have not demonstrated cause and effect. Still, the work is prompting calls for systematic studies combining GPS-based ionospheric monitoring with earthquake catalogs — an approach that could settle the question with actual data.

image.alt: Historical illustration of the sun bombarding and electrifying Earth
image.credit: Photo by Auckland War Memorial Museum on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Can solar storms trigger earthquakes?
Kyoto University: Could ionospheric disturbances influence earthquakes?
Live Science: Solar flares may be triggering earthquakes, controversial study claims

stories.headline: Cell Membrane Trick Could Triple the Power Output of Blue Energy
stories.hook: Researchers borrowed a structure from living cells to make ocean-to-river power generation finally competitive with conventional renewables.
stories.body:
Every second, somewhere in the world, a river meets the sea. Where fresh water mixes with salt water, a remarkable amount of energy is theoretically available: the chemical potential difference between the two solutions can drive ion flows through membranes and generate electricity. This is 'blue energy,' or osmotic power, and it's been a tantalizing but frustratingly inefficient renewable resource for decades. A new approach from researchers at EPFL in Switzerland may have finally cracked the efficiency problem.

The team's breakthrough was to coat the inside of nanopore membranes — the tiny channels through which ions flow — with lipid bilayers, the same double-layer of fat molecules that forms the outer wall of every living cell. These bilayers self-assemble spontaneously and create an ultra-thin film of bound water just a few molecules thick. This layer prevents ions from directly touching the nanopore walls, dramatically reducing friction and allowing them to slip through far more quickly and selectively. The result: a prototype membrane that produces roughly 15 watts per square meter, compared to 5 to 7 watts per square meter for the best existing polymer membranes — a two-to-three times improvement.

Blue energy has an inherent advantage that solar and wind lack: it's available continuously, day and night, wherever rivers discharge into the ocean — which accounts for about 150 major river mouths around the world. With improved efficiency, osmotic power plants sited at river estuaries could provide baseline renewable electricity to coastal cities. The EPFL researchers say the next step is scaling the lipid coating approach to large-format membranes suitable for industrial deployment.

image.alt: Stormy choppy ocean waves from above
image.credit: Photo by Nick Fewings on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Scientists create slippery nanopores that supercharge blue energy
EPFL: Slippery ions create a smoother path to blue energy

stories.headline: Seven New 'Frog-Like' Insects Discovered in a Ugandan Rainforest — One Named After a Mother
stories.hook: The first new Batracomorphus leafhoppers found in Africa since 1981 were hiding in high-altitude rainforest, and they look surprisingly like frogs.
stories.body:
Hidden in light traps set at altitudes above 1,500 meters in Uganda's Kibale National Park, a researcher from Anglia Ruskin University has discovered seven species of insect that have never been described by science. The new species belong to the genus Batracomorphus — Greek for 'frog-shaped' — a group of leafhoppers named for their large eyes, compact bodies, and powerful hind legs that give them an uncanny resemblance to tiny frogs. The discovery represents the first new Batracomorphus species recorded anywhere in Africa since 1981.

Dr. Alvin Helden, the entomologist who made the discoveries, had to get unusually intimate with the insects to classify them properly. The identification of leafhopper species relies heavily on the structure of their genitalia, which operates on a 'lock and key' principle: male genitalia can only couple with the corresponding female structures of the same species, preventing accidental cross-species mating. Under the microscope, the subtle differences in these structures revealed seven distinct evolutionary lineages that had previously been lumped together or overlooked entirely.

One of the new species, Batracomorphus ruthae, was named in memory of Dr. Helden's mother, Ruth, who passed away in 2022. She had, he noted, been a significant source of encouragement for his early interest in science. The discovery adds to a growing realization that African tropical forests — particularly at higher elevations — remain dramatically under-surveyed, and that new species are far more common than the public tends to assume. Scientists currently identify more than 16,000 new species per year across all domains of life.

image.alt: Brown and black grasshopper resting on a green leaf in the Peruvian rainforest
image.credit: Photo by Ulrike Langner on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Scientists discover seven strange frog-like insects hidden in Uganda's rainforest
The Brighter Side: Scientist discovers seven never-before-seen species of a frog-like insect

stories.headline: A Robotic Hand With Fingernail-Like Tips Can Now Peel Fruit and Open Lids
stories.hook: The humble fingernail turns out to be a surprisingly important piece of human dexterity — and robots just got a version of their own.
stories.body:
One of the longest-standing challenges in robotics is not lifting heavy objects or running at high speed — it's doing the mundane things human hands do effortlessly: peeling the sticker off a banana, prying open the lid of a jar, picking up a single sheet of paper from a flat surface. These tasks require fine edge sensitivity and the ability to slip under thin, flat objects, and they have stumped robot designers for decades. A new design described in research published this month introduces a solution borrowed from anatomy: fingernail-like rigid tips at the ends of flexible robotic fingers.

The nail-tipped design works by providing a hard, thin edge that can slide under objects without requiring the finger to grip from both sides simultaneously. Combined with soft, pressure-sensitive pads on the underside of the fingers, the design gives the robot something close to the precision grip that humans develop naturally. In tests, the system successfully peeled fruit, opened bottle caps and jar lids, and picked up thin flat objects — tasks that previous soft-robotic hands typically struggled with or failed at entirely.

The practical applications span manufacturing (handling fragile or irregularly shaped parts), healthcare (assistive devices for people with limited hand function), and domestic robotics (household robots that can actually be useful in a real kitchen). The research is part of a broader push to give robots tactile intelligence rather than just brute force, reflecting a growing consensus in the field that the future of useful robotics depends on machines that can feel and adapt, not just move fast.

image.alt: Yellow articulated humanoid robot with expressive hands on a white background
image.credit: Photo by Enchanted Tools on Unsplash

links.title: SciTechDaily: Science, Space and Technology News 2026

stories.headline: Cancer Cells Have a Unique 'Metabolic Fingerprint' on Their DNA — And It May Change Treatment
stories.hook: The same study that found energy enzymes living on human DNA also revealed that every cancer type has its own distinct enzymatic signature.
stories.body:
The discovery of metabolic enzymes on chromatin (see story #5 today) has a particularly striking implication for oncology: different cancers appear to have completely distinct patterns of which enzymes are present on their DNA. This 'nuclear metabolic fingerprint,' identified by researchers in the same Nature Communications study, could give doctors a powerful new way to classify cancers and predict how they will behave.

Cancers are already classified by genetic mutations, protein expression, and other molecular markers — but metabolism has been an underappreciated dimension. The new data suggests that cancer cells don't just use energy differently from healthy cells; they do so in a way that leaves a specific, readable imprint on their genome's physical structure. Triple-negative breast cancer, for example, had a distinct fingerprint from other breast cancer subtypes. Colorectal cancers clustered separately from pancreatic cancers. The patterns appeared consistent enough to potentially serve as diagnostic markers.

Perhaps more clinically exciting is the functional observation: some of the enzymes congregate around sites of DNA damage in cancer cells. This means the 'mini metabolism' in the nucleus isn't just a bystander — it's actively involved in how cancer cells repair their DNA, which is directly relevant to how they respond to chemotherapy and radiation. If drugs can disrupt the delivery of specific enzymes to damaged DNA in cancer cells without affecting healthy cells, an entirely new class of targeted therapies could be on the horizon.

image.alt: Colorized microscope image of human colorectal cancer cells with blue nuclei and colored DNA markers
image.credit: Photo by National Cancer Institute on Unsplash

links.title: Medical Xpress: Cancer has a unique nuclear metabolic fingerprint, researchers discover
EurekAlert: Cancer has a unique nuclear metabolic fingerprint

stories.headline: Meta's Next AI Model Is Delayed — and the Company Is Talking to Google About Licensing Gemini
stories.hook: Even the world's most well-resourced AI lab is finding it hard to keep up with the pace of the field it helped create.
stories.body:
Meta's next flagship AI model, internally code-named Avocado, will not ship in March as originally planned, according to reporting from Fortune and other outlets. The model has been pushed to at least May 2026 after testing revealed it underperformed competitors from Google and OpenAI on key benchmarks. The delay is significant not just for Meta's product roadmap but for what it says about the pace of AI development industry-wide — even with billions of dollars in GPU infrastructure, staying ahead is genuinely difficult.

What's more striking is what appears to be happening behind the scenes: Meta is reportedly in discussions with Google about the possibility of temporarily licensing Gemini models while it works to improve its own. This would be an unusual arrangement for two companies that compete directly across AI assistants, advertising, and enterprise software — and it underscores the degree to which the competitive landscape has shifted. For years, Meta's open-source strategy (releasing model weights publicly) was a way of staying competitive with fewer resources; the Avocado situation suggests even that approach has limits.

Meanwhile, Meta announced four new generations of custom AI chips that it plans to deploy across its data centers by the end of 2027, and Atlassian this week laid off roughly 10% of its global workforce to redirect resources toward AI development. The industry's consolidation around AI is accelerating, and the companies that can close the compute gap fastest — through chips, data, or model architecture breakthroughs — are increasingly pulling ahead of everyone else.

image.alt: Rows of data servers in a white and gray server room

links.title: Fortune: Meta's Avocado AI delay, Adobe CEO change; Google Maps gets Gemini
TechStartups: Top Tech News Today, March 13, 2026

stories.headline: Yann LeCun Just Raised $1 Billion to Prove That Language Models Are the Wrong Approach to AI
stories.hook: The Turing Award winner who invented the convolutional neural network has bet his new startup on a radically different vision of intelligence.
stories.body:
Yann LeCun — one of the three 'godfathers' of deep learning, a Turing Award winner, and for years Meta's chief AI scientist — has been publicly arguing for years that large language models are fundamentally limited and cannot, on their own, lead to truly intelligent machines. Now he's put a billion dollars behind that argument. AMI Labs, the Paris-based startup LeCun co-founded after leaving Meta, closed a $1.03 billion seed round on March 10, 2026, at a $3.5 billion pre-money valuation — the largest seed round in European startup history.

AMI is building 'world models': AI systems that learn to predict and understand the physical world by observing it, rather than by predicting the next word in a text string. LeCun's Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA), the theoretical framework underlying the company's approach, is designed to let AI reason about causality, plan multi-step actions, and develop common-sense physical understanding — capabilities that current LLMs demonstrably struggle with. Target applications include industrial automation, robotics, and healthcare, where the gap between LLM capability and real-world physical reasoning is most consequential.

LeCun is not the only researcher pursuing world models — DeepMind, OpenAI, and several academic groups have similar research programs — but AMI's seed raise signals that sophisticated investors believe there is genuine commercial potential in the approach, not just academic interest. The company has no products, no revenue, and by LeCun's own admission will spend its first year entirely on research. The investors appear to be betting on the person and the idea, not a near-term business.

image.alt: Rack of servers in a modern server room with blue lighting
image.credit: Photo by Kevin Ache on Unsplash

links.title: TechCrunch: Yann LeCun's AMI Labs raises $1.03B to build world models
The Next Web: Yann LeCun just raised $1bn to prove the AI industry has got it wrong
Crunchbase: Turing Winner LeCun's New 'World Model' AI Lab Raises $1B in Europe's Largest Seed Round

stories.headline: Mars May Have Stayed Habitable Billions of Years Longer Than Scientists Thought
stories.hook: Ancient sand dunes in Gale Crater appear to have been soaked by underground water at a time when Mars was supposed to be a frozen, dead world.
stories.body:
Scientists have long believed that Mars lost most of its liquid water around 3 billion years ago, after its magnetic field collapsed and its thin atmosphere was gradually stripped away by solar wind. But a growing accumulation of evidence from the Curiosity rover is pushing that timeline forward in a way that could have major implications for the question of whether Mars was ever habitable long enough for life to gain a foothold.

Recent analysis of ancient sand dune deposits in Gale Crater suggests they were thoroughly soaked by underground water at a time that would fall well after Mars was supposed to have gone completely dry. The water appears to have percolated up from below rather than falling as rain or flowing in rivers — a pattern consistent with a deep subsurface hydrosphere that persisted long after surface water vanished. Clay minerals and carbonates detected in the sediments are the chemical fingerprints of prolonged water-rock interaction, not brief or superficial wetting.

The picture that's emerging is of a Mars with a complex, multi-chapter hydrological history — not a simple story of early wetness followed by permanent desiccation, but a planet where water periodically migrated between the surface, the subsurface, and the atmosphere over billions of years. For astrobiologists, this is enormously interesting: organisms that evolved in near-surface environments could potentially have retreated deeper underground as surface conditions worsened, surviving in protected niches for far longer than the conventional model would allow. Whether such organisms actually existed on Mars remains completely unknown, but the window of opportunity appears larger than previously imagined.

image.alt: Aerial view of barren desert craters resembling Mars surface terrain
image.credit: Photo by Anton Repponen on Unsplash

links.title: Discover Magazine: Giant Spiderweb Formations on Mars Contain Ancient Evidence of the Planet's Waterlogged Past
Scientific American: These Are the Most Exciting Space Science Events for 2026

stories.headline: Atlassian Cut 10% of Its Staff to Go All-In on AI — and It Won't Be the Last Tech Company to Do So
stories.hook: The maker of Jira and Confluence is the latest company to restructure aggressively around AI, replacing its CTO with two AI-focused ones in the process.
stories.body:
Atlassian, the Australian enterprise software company behind Jira, Confluence, and Trello, announced this week that it is laying off approximately 10% of its global workforce — roughly 1,200 people — in order to redirect resources toward artificial intelligence development. The company also replaced its Chief Technology Officer with two new AI-focused CTOs, a structural shift that signals how fundamentally leadership views the transition underway. The layoffs and restructuring bring the total number of tech sector job cuts in March 2026 to approximately 45,000, according to trackers discussed on Hacker News.

The move is part of a broader pattern playing out across the industry: companies that built their workforces for a pre-AI product development model are realizing that smaller, more AI-augmented teams can ship comparable or better products. Atlassian's products are particularly well-suited to AI enhancement — project management, documentation, and code review are exactly the kinds of structured knowledge work that current AI systems can meaningfully accelerate.

For the workers displaced, the rationale offers little comfort, but the industrial logic is clear. Amazon this week announced an additional €18 billion investment in Spanish data center capacity through 2035 to support AI workloads. Singapore and South Korea formalized an AI development alliance with a $300 million joint fund. Microsoft 365 is rolling out AI-powered features across Teams and other collaboration tools in March. Every major enterprise software company is in a race to embed AI deeply enough that switching costs rise — and the companies that establish that foothold earliest will be very difficult to displace.

image.alt: Server racks in a dimly lit modern data center corridor
image.credit: Photo by Kevin Ache on Unsplash

links.title: TechStartups: Top Tech News Today, March 13, 2026
MIT Technology Review: The Download — Protesting AI, and what's floating in space
